back: Anything that has mass and occupies space.
chapter: Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

front: Difference between a pure substance and a mixture?
back: Pure substance has fixed composition (element or compound); mixture is two+ substances physically combined, separable by physical means.
chapter: Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

back: A substance that cannot be broken down into simpler substances by chemical means.
chapter: Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

back: Two or more elements chemically bonded in a fixed ratio.
chapter: Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

back: Homogeneous is uniform throughout (a solution); heterogeneous has visibly distinct regions.
chapter: Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

front: Give an example of a homogeneous mixture.
back: Salt water or air — uniform composition.
chapter: Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

front: Give an example of a heterogeneous mixture.
back: Italian dressing or sand in water — distinct phases.
chapter: Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

front: Are diamond and graphite elements, compounds, or mixtures?
chapter: Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

back: Homogeneous mixture (a solid solution alloy of copper and zinc).
chapter: Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

back: Compound — CO2 with a fixed C:O ratio.
chapter: Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

back: Physical: identity unchanged (state/shape). Chemical: new substance forms.
chapter: Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

front: Is boiling water physical or chemical?
chapter: Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

front: Is rusting iron physical or chemical?
back: Chemical — forms iron oxide, a new substance.
chapter: Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

front: Is dissolving sugar physical or chemical?
back: Physical — sugar can be recovered by evaporation.
chapter: Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

front: Name four signs of a chemical change.
back: Color change, gas/bubbles, precipitate, temperature/light change.
chapter: Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

back: Physical: observable without changing identity (density, color). Chemical: describes reactivity (flammability).
chapter: Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

back: Chemical property — describes how it burns/reacts.
chapter: Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

back: Physical property — measured without changing identity.
chapter: Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

back: Extensive depends on amount (mass, volume); intensive does not (density, temperature).

back: No — leading zeros are never significant.

back: Yes — zeros between nonzero digits always count.

back: Ambiguous, 2 to 4; write in scientific notation to clarify.

Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

back: Answer keeps the fewest significant figures of any factor.
chapter: Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

back: Answer keeps the fewest decimal places.

Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

front: How many sig figs does an exact counted number have?
back: Infinite — exact numbers don't limit sig figs.
chapter: Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)
Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)
Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

back: Closeness of a measurement to the true/accepted value.
chapter: Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

back: Closeness of repeated measurements to each other (reproducibility).
chapter: Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

front: Tightly clustered but far from true value = ?
chapter: Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

back: Systematic error (consistent bias in one direction).

Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

front: Why does ice float on water?
back: Solid water (ice) is less dense than liquid water.
chapter: Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

front: How is volume of an irregular solid measured?
back: Water displacement — final minus initial volume.

front: At what temperature are C and F equal?
chapter: Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

front: Do Celsius and Kelvin degrees have the same size?
back: Yes — a change of 1 C equals a change of 1 K.
chapter: Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

back: Converting units by multiplying by conversion factors so unwanted units cancel.

back: Qualitative describes (color); quantitative measures with numbers and units.
chapter: Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

back: Hypothesis: testable guess. Theory: well-tested explanation. Law: summary of consistent behavior.
chapter: Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

front: Does a scientific law explain why?
back: No — a law summarizes what happens; a theory explains why.
chapter: Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

front: Where do you read a graduated cylinder?
back: Bottom of the meniscus at eye level.
chapter: Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

front: Which glassware delivers a precise fixed volume?

front: Edge length of a gold cube (d=19.3), mass 96.5 g?
back: V = 5.00 cm^3; edge = cube root = 1.71 cm.

Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

front: Why can subtracting two close numbers lose precision?
back: The difference has fewer significant figures than either original value.
chapter: Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)
Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

front: Best unit to report a solid metal's density?
chapter: Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

front: Mass of 250.0 mL of liquid, d = 0.7893 g/mL?
chapter: Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)
Chapter 1: Chemical Foundations (Matter, Measurement & Problem Solving)

front: State the law of conservation of mass.
back: Mass is neither created nor destroyed in a chemical reaction.

front: State the law of definite proportions.
back: A given compound always contains the same elements in the same mass ratio.

front: State the law of multiple proportions.
back: When two elements form multiple compounds, the masses of one per fixed mass of the other are in small whole-number ratios.

front: Name the four postulates of Dalton's atomic theory (summary).
back: Elements are made of atoms; atoms of an element are identical; compounds form in fixed ratios; reactions rearrange atoms.

front: Which Dalton postulate is now known to be false?
back: Atoms of an element are all identical (isotopes differ in mass); atoms are indivisible.
The electron (via cathode ray tube), showing atoms have negative particles.

front: What was Thomson's model of the atom called?
What did Millikan's oil-drop experiment measure?
What did Rutherford's gold-foil experiment show?
back: A small, dense, positive nucleus; most of the atom is empty space.

front: Charge and relative mass of a proton?
Charge and relative mass of a neutron?
Charge and relative mass of an electron?
Where is nearly all the atom's mass located?
What defines the identity of an element?
back: Its number of protons (atomic number, Z).
The number of protons in the nucleus.
Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons (same Z, different mass number).

front: Do isotopes have the same chemical properties?
back: Yes — same number of protons and electrons; only mass differs.

front: Isotope notation: what do superscript and subscript mean?
back: Superscript = mass number; subscript = atomic number.

front: How many protons, neutrons, electrons in neutral 35-Cl?

back: The weighted average of isotope masses by their fractional abundances.

front: An element's average mass is 63.5 with isotopes 63 and 65. Which is more abundant?
back: The 63 isotope — average is closer to 63.

front: How does a mass spectrometer work (concept)?
back: Ionizes atoms, separates ions by mass-to-charge ratio, and records relative abundances.
Species with the same number of electrons.

front: Are K+ and Ca2+ isoelectronic? How many electrons?
Between K+ and Ca2+, which has the smaller radius and why?
back: Ca2+ — more protons (20 vs 19) pull the same 18 electrons tighter.

front: Metals tend to form which type of ion?
Nonmetals tend to form which type of ion?

back: A charged group of covalently bonded atoms acting as one unit.

front: Formula and charge of bicarbonate (hydrogen carbonate).

back: Nitrate NO3-, nitrite NO2- (one fewer oxygen).
A compound of cations and anions held by electrostatic attraction.

front: How do you write an ionic formula?
back: Balance charges to net zero; use subscripts (criss-cross), reduce to lowest ratio.

front: When are parentheses used in a formula?
back: When more than one polyatomic ion is present, e.g., (NH4)3PO4.

front: How do you name a binary ionic compound?
back: Cation name + anion root + -ide (e.g., sodium chloride).

front: When do you use Roman numerals in naming?
back: For transition metals with variable charge, to show the cation charge.

front: How do you name a binary molecular (covalent) compound?
back: Use prefixes (mono, di, tri...) for each element; second ends in -ide.

front: Do you use 'mono-' on the first element?
back: No — 'mono' is omitted on the first element.

front: How are binary acids named (H + nonmetal)?
back: hydro- + root + -ic acid (e.g., HCl = hydrochloric acid).

front: How are oxoacids from -ate ions named?
How are oxoacids from -ite ions named?

back: Two or more atoms covalently bonded as a discrete unit.

front: What is a diatomic element? Name them.
back: Elements that exist as 2-atom molecules: H2, N2, O2, F2, Cl2, Br2, I2.
A notation showing the elements and number of each atom in a substance.
Empirical is the simplest whole-number ratio; molecular gives the actual atom counts.
Shows how atoms are connected (bonds), not just counts.
An ionic compound with water molecules incorporated in its structure (e.g., CuSO4.5H2O).

front: Reading the periodic table: what are periods?

back: Vertical columns with similar chemical properties.
Left and center of the periodic table.
Upper right of the periodic table.
Elements along the metal/nonmetal staircase with intermediate properties (e.g., Si, As).

front: Ion an atom forms with 16 p, 16 n, 18 e?
Symbol for ion with 24 p, 28 n, 21 e?
What is the atomic mass unit (amu) based on?
back: 1/12 the mass of a carbon-12 atom.
The amount of substance containing Avogadro's number of particles.
The mass of one mole of a substance (g/mol), numerically equal to the atomic/formula mass.

front: How do you find the molar mass of a compound?
back: Sum the molar masses of all atoms in the formula.

front: Convert moles to number of particles.
How many molecules in 2.0 mol H2O?
How many moles in 36.0 g of water?
How many oxygen atoms in 1 mol CO2?

back: The mass percent of each element in a compound.

front: Formula for mass percent of an element.
back: (mass of element in 1 mol / molar mass) x 100%.
The simplest whole-number ratio of atoms.

front: Steps to find empirical formula from percent composition?
back: Assume 100 g, convert to moles, divide by smallest, round to whole numbers.

front: Empirical formula of 40.0% C, 6.7% H, 53.3% O?
How do you get molecular formula from empirical?
back: Divide molar mass by empirical mass; multiply subscripts by that integer.

front: What is combustion analysis used for?
back: Finding empirical formulas of C-H-O compounds from CO2 and H2O produced.

front: In combustion analysis, how do you find mol C?
back: mol C = mol CO2 (all carbon becomes CO2).

front: In combustion analysis, how do you find mol H?
In combustion analysis, how do you find mass O?
back: Subtract mass of C and H from the sample mass.

front: What does a balanced equation conserve?
back: Atoms of each element (and mass and charge).

front: Balance combustion of butane: coefficient of O2?

back: The ratio of coefficients between two species in a balanced equation.

front: For N2 + 3H2 -> 2NH3, mol H2 to make 6 mol NH3?
For 2KClO3 -> 2KCl + 3O2, mol O2 from 4 mol KClO3?
Steps for a mass-to-mass stoichiometry problem?
back: g A -> mol A -> mol B (ratio) -> g B.

front: Mass of AlCl3 from 5.40 g Al (excess Cl2), 2Al+3Cl2->2AlCl3?

back: The reactant that runs out first and limits product formed.
The reactant left over after the reaction stops.

front: How do you identify the limiting reagent?
back: Convert each reactant to product moles; the one giving less product is limiting.

front: N2 + 3H2 -> 2NH3 with 0.29 mol N2 and 1.5 mol H2 — limiting?
back: N2 (needs 0.86 mol H2; H2 is in excess).

front: How do you find leftover excess reagent mass?
back: Subtract the amount consumed by the limiting reagent from the starting amount.
The maximum product predicted from the limiting reagent.
The amount of product actually obtained experimentally.

front: Actual 18.5 g, theoretical 22.0 g — percent yield?
If percent yield is 78% and theoretical is 50.0 g, actual yield?
Why is actual yield usually less than theoretical?
back: Side reactions, incomplete reactions, and loss during recovery.

front: Can percent yield exceed 100%? What would it indicate?
back: Not truly — it signals impure product or measurement error.

front: For 2 Al + 3 CuCl2, 0.20 mol Al and 0.26 mol CuCl2 — limiting?
back: CuCl2 (Al needs 0.30 mol CuCl2; only 0.26 available).
Formula weight applies to ionic (formula units); molecular weight to molecules; both are sums of atomic masses.
The simplest ratio unit of an ionic compound (e.g., NaCl).

front: Moles in 100. g of CaCO3 (100.09 g/mol)?
Grams of CaCO3 to make 11.0 g CO2 (CaCO3->CaO+CO2)?
How many moles of atoms in 1 mole of O2?
Mass of 3.01 x 10^23 molecules of H2O?
Which has more atoms: 1 mol He or 1 mol O2?
back: Equal molecules, but O2 has 2x the atoms (2 mol atoms vs 1).

front: Percent by mass of water in a 50.0 g sample with 20.0 g water?
Mole-to-mole ratio in 2 H2O2 -> 2 H2O + O2 for O2 per H2O2?
For CH4 + 2O2 -> CO2 + 2H2O, mol H2O per mol CH4?
If 16 g CH4 (1 mol) reacts with 16 g O2 (0.5 mol), which is limiting?
back: O2 — needs 2 mol per mol CH4; only 0.5 mol present.
The quantitative relationship between reactants and products in a reaction.

front: Number of moles in 6.022 x 10^24 particles?
How many H atoms in 1 mol of CH4?
A compound is 52.2% C, 13.0% H, 34.8% O — empirical?
Molecular formula if empirical C2H6O and molar mass 46?
Sum rule: total mass of reactants vs products?
If 10.0 g reactant fully reacts, total product mass?
For 4NH3+5O2->4NO+6H2O, mol NO from 10 mol O2?
Grams of O2 needed to burn 1 mol CH4 (CH4+2O2)?
What is a spectator in a stoichiometry sense (mass basis)?
back: A species present but not consumed; its mass is unchanged.

front: Define molar volume of an ideal gas at STP.
Moles in 44.8 L of gas at STP?
How many formula units in 1 mol NaCl?
Ratio of coefficients = ratio of what?
A hydrocarbon is 82.7% C, 17.3% H — empirical?
If a sample yields 0.0375 mol CO2, mass of C?
Why must an equation be balanced before stoichiometry?
back: Mole ratios come from coefficients; unbalanced ratios give wrong amounts.
Moles of each reactant -> product moles via ratio -> smaller value is limiting -> compute yield.

front: For 2H2+O2->2H2O, grams of water from 4.0 g H2 (excess O2)?

back: Equal volumes of gases at same T,P contain equal numbers of molecules.

front: Density of a gas relates to molar mass how (at STP)?

back: A solution in which water is the solvent.
Solute is dissolved; solvent does the dissolving (present in greater amount).
A substance that conducts electricity when dissolved (produces ions).
Strong fully ionizes; weak only partially ionizes.

front: Give an example of a nonelectrolyte.
back: Sugar (glucose) or ethanol — dissolves without forming ions.

front: Are strong acids strong or weak electrolytes?
Are soluble ionic salts strong or weak electrolytes?
Why does water dissolve ionic compounds?
back: Its polarity lets it surround and stabilize ions (hydration).
Moles of solute per liter of solution (mol/L).

front: Molarity of 5.85 g NaCl in 250 mL?
Moles of solute in 2.0 L of 0.50 M solution?
Grams of NaCl in 500 mL of 1.0 M solution?
Dilute 25.0 mL of 0.500 M to 0.100 M — final volume?
Water to add diluting 25.0 mL 0.500 M to 0.100 M?
Does dilution change moles of solute?
back: No — only volume/concentration change; moles stay constant.
A reaction forming an insoluble solid (precipitate) from mixed solutions.
An insoluble solid that forms and separates from solution.

front: Solubility rule: are all nitrates soluble?
Solubility rule: are Group 1 and ammonium salts soluble?
Solubility rule for chlorides, bromides, iodides?
back: Soluble except with Ag+, Pb2+, Hg2 2+.
Soluble except with Ba2+, Pb2+, Ca2+ (slightly), Sr2+.
Mostly insoluble except Group 1 and Ba2+ (Ca2+ slightly).

front: Solubility rule for carbonates and phosphates?
back: Mostly insoluble except Group 1 and ammonium.
An equation showing complete neutral formulas of all species.

front: What is a complete ionic equation?
back: All soluble strong electrolytes written as separated ions.

front: What is a net ionic equation?
back: The equation with spectator ions removed, showing only reacting species.
Ions that appear unchanged on both sides and are omitted from the net ionic equation.
A substance that produces H+ (H3O+) in water.
A substance that produces OH- in water.
HCl, HNO3, H2SO4 (also HBr, HI, HClO4).

front: Net ionic for a strong acid + strong base neutralization?

back: Adding a solution of known concentration to determine an unknown concentration.
Where moles of acid equal moles of base (stoichiometrically complete).

front: Volume of 0.150 M NaOH to neutralize 30.0 mL of 0.200 M HCl?

back: OIL RIG: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain.
The species that gets reduced (it takes electrons).
The species that gets oxidized (it gives electrons).

front: Oxidation number of a free element?
Oxidation number of a monatomic ion?
Sum of oxidation numbers in a neutral compound?
Sum of oxidation numbers in a polyatomic ion?
Oxidation number of Mn in MnO4-?
Oxidation number of Cr in Cr2O7 2-?
Oxidation number of S in SO4 2-?
In MnO4- -> Mn2+, is Mn oxidized or reduced?

back: Two or more reactants form one product (A + B -> AB).
One reactant breaks into two or more products.
One element displaces another in a compound (A + BC -> AC + B).
Ions exchange partners (AB + CD -> AD + CB), often forming a precipitate.
A substance reacts with O2, releasing heat (hydrocarbons -> CO2 + H2O).
Using molarity and volume to relate moles in reactions in solution.

front: Moles of HCl in 30.0 mL of 0.200 M?
Mass of AgCl from 25.0 mL of 0.100 M AgNO3 (excess Cl-)?
Concentration of Cl- from 0.0100 mol in 100.0 mL?
What is the activity series used for?
back: Predicting whether a single-replacement reaction occurs (more active displaces less active).

front: Will Zn displace Cu2+ from solution?
back: Yes — Zn is more active than Cu.
No — Cu is less active than Zn.
A reaction showing only oxidation or only reduction with electrons.

front: Why do we write strong acids as ions in ionic equations?
Why write weak acids as molecules in ionic equations?
back: They only partially ionize; mostly exist as intact molecules.

front: Is HC2H3O2 (acetic acid) strong or weak?
Is NH3 a strong or weak base?
What color change indicates an acid-base endpoint (phenolphthalein)?
What determines a good titration indicator?
back: One that changes color near the equivalence-point pH.
The amount of solute per amount of solution (or solvent).

front: What happens to concentration when you add solvent?
Moles of ions from 1 mol of CaCl2 dissolving?
Total ion concentration of 0.10 M CaCl2?
Why do soluble ionic compounds conduct electricity in water?
Is a precipitate a strong, weak, or non-electrolyte in the equation?
back: Written as a solid (s), not as ions.

front: General form of an acid-base neutralization product besides water?
back: A salt (ionic compound of the cation and anion).

front: What is the driving force in precipitation reactions?
back: Formation of an insoluble solid removes ions from solution.

front: How many mL of water added to make 0.60 M into 0.40 M for 50 mL?
Molarity if 0.25 mol dissolved to 500 mL?
Convert 250 mL of 2.0 M to moles.

back: One holding the maximum solute that can dissolve at that temperature.

front: Redox: assign and check Mn in KMnO4 to MnSO4 change.
What is a strong electrolyte solution's conductivity vs weak?
back: Strong conducts well (many ions); weak conducts poorly (few ions).

front: What instrument measures gas pressure in a container?

back: At constant T and n, P and V are inversely proportional: P1V1 = P2V2.
At constant P and n, V is directly proportional to T (in K): V1/T1 = V2/T2.
At constant V and n, P is directly proportional to T (in K): P1/T1 = P2/T2.
At constant T and P, V is proportional to n (moles): V1/n1 = V2/n2.

front: Why must temperature be in Kelvin for gas laws?
back: The laws require an absolute scale; 0 K corresponds to zero volume/pressure extrapolation.

front: Boyle: if V halves at constant T, what happens to P?
Charles: gas at 2.0 L, 300 K heated to 600 K — new volume?
What does STP stand for and its values?
back: Standard Temperature and Pressure: 0 C (273.15 K) and 1 atm.

front: Molar volume of an ideal gas at STP?
Volume of 2.00 mol of gas at STP?
Moles in 11.2 L of gas at STP?
Find P: 1.0 mol gas, 22.4 L, 273 K.
What are the assumptions of kinetic molecular theory?
back: Tiny particles, negligible volume, no attractions, elastic collisions, KE proportional to T.

front: Average kinetic energy of a gas depends on what?
Do all gases at the same T have the same average KE?
back: Yes — average KE depends only on temperature.

front: Relationship of molecular speed to molar mass at same T?
back: Lighter molecules move faster (speed inversely related to sqrt of molar mass).

front: Which moves faster at the same T: He or O2?

back: Rate of effusion is inversely proportional to sqrt of molar mass.

front: Which effuses faster, H2 or CO2?

back: Escape of gas molecules through a tiny hole into a vacuum.
Spreading/mixing of gas molecules through one another.

front: State Dalton's law of partial pressures.
back: Total pressure = sum of partial pressures of each gas.
Moles of a component / total moles.

front: A gas collected over water — how to get dry gas pressure?
back: Subtract the vapor pressure of water from total pressure.

front: Partial pressure of gas that is 0.25 mole fraction at 780 mmHg total?
Formula relating density and molar mass of a gas.
Gas density at STP from molar mass?
Molar mass from gas density: M = ?

back: A hypothetical gas that perfectly obeys PV=nRT (no volume, no attractions).

front: When do real gases deviate from ideal behavior?
back: At high pressure and low temperature.

front: Why do real gases deviate at high pressure?
back: Molecular volume becomes significant relative to container volume.

front: Why do real gases deviate at low temperature?
back: Intermolecular attractions become significant as molecules slow down.

front: Which gas behaves most ideally: He or NH3? Why?
Name the equation that corrects for real gas behavior.
In van der Waals, what does 'a' correct for?
In van der Waals, what does 'b' correct for?
Gas stoichiometry: how to relate volume to moles at STP?
Volume of O2 (STP) to burn 1 mol CH4 (CH4+2O2)?
At constant n and T, P vs V graph shape?
At constant n and P, V vs T (K) graph shape?
back: A straight line through the origin.

front: If T doubles at constant V and n, what happens to P?
A 5.00 L container at 2.00 atm expands to 10.0 L (constant T). New P?
Gas at 25 C, 1.0 atm heated to 50 C at constant V. Approx new P?

back: The pressure a gas in a mixture would exert alone in the same volume.

front: If you double the moles of gas at constant T and V, pressure does what?
Standard molar volume applies to which conditions?
Which variable must always be absolute in gas law calcs?
What is the pressure unit 'bar' vs atm?
Express PV = nRT solved for V.
What does a larger molar mass mean for effusion rate?
KMT: are collisions between ideal gas particles elastic or inelastic?
KMT: what is the assumed volume of ideal gas particles?
As temperature rises, molecular speed distribution does what?
back: Shifts to higher speeds and broadens (Maxwell-Boltzmann).

front: Two gases at same T: which has broader speed distribution?
back: The lighter gas (higher, more spread speeds).

front: A balloon at constant P is heated. What happens to volume?
Why does a sealed can burst when heated?
back: Pressure rises with temperature at constant volume (Gay-Lussac).
Each partial pressure is proportional to that gas's moles.

front: Mole fraction of O2 if P(O2)=0.21 atm and total=1.00 atm?
Molar mass of a gas with density 1.34 g/L at STP?
What law predicts equal volumes have equal molecules (same T,P)?
A gas sample doubles in volume and halves in pressure — T constant?

back: Yes — for an ideal gas the compressibility factor Z = 1.

front: Rank average speed: He, Ne, Ar at same T.
What happens to gas volume if both P doubles and T doubles?
Partial pressure of N2 in air (~78%) at 1.00 atm?
Why is water vapor pressure subtracted when collecting gas over water?
back: The measured total includes water vapor; subtract it for the dry gas.

front: Gas constant R links which four quantities?
back: P, V, n, T in PV=nRT.

front: Volume of 1.00 mol ideal gas at 273 K, 1 atm?
If you compress a gas to half volume at constant T, KE changes?
back: No — KE depends only on T, which is constant.

front: Behavior comparison: high T favors ideal or real deviations?
Manometer: how is gas pressure found relative to atmosphere?
back: Compare mercury heights; add or subtract the height difference from atmospheric.

front: Two flasks equal V and T, one H2 one O2 at same P — equal moles?
back: Yes — same P, V, T means equal moles (Avogadro).

front: Two flasks equal V, T, P of H2 and O2 — equal mass?
back: No — O2 is 16x heavier per molecule, so greater mass.

front: What is the compressibility factor Z?
back: Z = PV/nRT; equals 1 for ideal gases.

front: Effusion rate ratio of H2 to O2?
A rigid container's gas is cooled. Pressure does what?
Why can gases be compressed easily?
back: Large spaces between particles (mostly empty space).

front: Why do gases fill their container completely?
back: Particles move freely and randomly with negligible attractions.
